required.title: [Read Aloud] The Story of the World, Volume 4: The Modern Age
required.author: Susan Wise Bauer

required.title: [Read Aloud] The Diary of a Young Girl
required.author: Anne Frank (selections)

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Sadako and the Thousand Paper Cranes
suggested.author: Eleanor Coerr

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] The Yellow Star: The Legend of King Christian X of Denmark
suggested.author: Carmen Agra Deedy

suggested.title: [Look Together] DK Eyewitness: World War II
[Look Together] Maps of WWI and WWII

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Bloodlands: Europe Between Hitler and Stalin
supplementary.author: Timothy Snyder

competence_markers.description: The student can narrate the basic story of World War I — what caused it, what the fighting was like, and how it ended
The student can narrate the rise of Hitler and the step-by-step process of the Holocaust — from laws to ghettos to death camps
The student can tell the story of Anne Frank and explain what happened to her and why
The student is aware of Soviet atrocities — the gulags, the Holodomor, the Great Terror — and can explain that atrocity was not limited to one political side
The student can name at least one genocide beyond the Holocaust (Porajmos, Armenian Genocide, Holodomor) and explain why it receives less attention
The student can narrate the story of the atomic bombings and articulate why the bomb changed the world permanently
The student understands the concept of total war — that twentieth-century wars targeted entire societies, not just armies
The student demonstrates emotional engagement with the material — genuine feeling — without shutting down or retreating into abstraction
The student can articulate at least one lesson from this period about the danger of unchecked government power

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story of how World War I started.
narration_prompts.detail: A man shot an archduke — and then the whole world went to war. How did that happen? What was the war like for the soldiers in the trenches?
What happened to the Jewish people in Europe? How did it happen — not all at once, but step by step? Tell me about Anne Frank.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about an atrocity committed by the Soviet Union.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened during the Holodomor? Or in the gulags? How was it different from what the Nazis did? How was it similar?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a genocide that most people have never heard of.
narration_prompts.detail: The Porajmos, the Armenian Genocide, or the Holodomor. What happened? Why don't more people know about it?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story of the atomic bomb.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened at Hiroshima? What happened to Sadako? What did the bomb change about the world forever?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why did these terrible things happen?
narration_prompts.detail: Pick one — the Holocaust, the Holodomor, the Great Leap Forward — and explain not just what happened but why. What ideas made people think this was acceptable?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the most important thing you learned from this packet?
narration_prompts.detail: What will you remember? What do you think people should never forget?

cross_connections.description: The nationalism, industrial technology, and ideological movements that produced the world wars all originated in the period covered by 1.1.4. The student who understands the French Revolution's trajectory from idealism to terror is prepared to see the same pattern in the Bolshevik Revolution.

The ideologies of the twentieth century — fascism, communism, nationalism — function as secular religions with their own creation myths, their own saints, and their own heretics. 1.3.1 provides the framework for understanding how belief systems shape human behavior.

The failure of democratic institutions in the interwar period — how democracies gave way to dictatorships — connects directly to 1.4.1's exploration of what makes government legitimate and what happens when legitimacy collapses.

The geography of the world wars — the Western Front's trench terrain, Russia's vast distances, Japan's island geography, the Pacific theater — connects directly to geographic literacy. Wars are fought on real ground.

The moral questions raised by this packet — the banality of evil, the courage of rescuers, the question of when obedience becomes complicity — are among the most important moral questions a human being can face.

The twentieth century is the ultimate case study in what happens when ideologies capture states. The student who understands how fascism and communism actually functioned is inoculated against their descendants.

The pattern of civilizational overreach, collapse, and renewal is visible in concentrated form in the twentieth century. The treaty that ended WWI created the conditions for WWII — the clearest example of how solutions create the next generation of problems.

content.why_this_matters:
You are going to learn about the most destructive period in all of human history. In the twentieth century — less than a hundred years ago — wars killed more people than had ever been killed in any previous century. Entire cities were burned to the ground. Entire peoples were marked for murder by their own governments. Weapons were invented that could destroy everything.

This is hard material. Some of it will be upsetting to hear. That is the correct response — it should be upsetting, because what happened was genuinely terrible. But you need to know it, because these things did not happen in some distant past. They happened to your great-grandparents' generation. The world you live in was shaped directly by these events. The borders on your map, the alliances between nations, the weapons that exist, the international laws that were written — all of it comes from this period.

You will learn this history as stories of real people — soldiers who went to war expecting glory and found horror, families torn apart by governments that decided they did not deserve to live, children who survived things no child should have to survive. You will also learn that the evil in this period did not come from only one side. Multiple governments, with completely different ideas about how the world should work, committed atrocities against their own people and against others. Understanding that is one of the most important lessons in this entire curriculum.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **An honest understanding of the scale of twentieth-century violence** — what happened, to whom, and why it matters
2. **The reality that atrocity is not limited to one political side** — fascism, communism, and democratic nations all committed terrible acts
3. **The Holocaust as a specific, deliberate, industrialized genocide** — not an abstraction but something done to real people through identifiable steps
4. **The existence of other genocides that receive less attention** — the Holodomor, the Armenian Genocide, the Porajmos — and the beginning of understanding why some atrocities are remembered and others are not
5. **The concept of total war** — when a war involves not just armies but entire societies, including civilians
6. **The atomic bomb as a turning point in human history** — the moment humans gained the power to destroy everything
7. **Emotional resilience in the face of hard history** — the capacity to learn about terrible things honestly without being overwhelmed or shutting down
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The World Before the Wars — Why Europe Was a Powder Keg

By 1900, Europe was the most powerful collection of nations on Earth. European countries controlled most of the world's territory through their empires — Britain, France, Germany, Russia, Austria-Hungary, the Ottoman Empire. They had the most advanced technology, the biggest armies, the most productive factories.

They were also deeply afraid of each other. Each major nation had built massive armies and navies. They had formed alliances — agreements that if one country was attacked, its allies would join the fight. Germany was allied with Austria-Hungary and the Ottoman Empire. France was allied with Russia and Britain. These alliances were supposed to prevent war by making war too costly. Instead, they guaranteed that any small war would become a giant one.

The nations of Europe also believed things about themselves that made war seem noble. Nationalism — the fierce pride in your own country and the belief that your nation was superior — was at its peak. Young men were taught that dying for your country was the highest honor. Generals planned for the next war using strategies from the last one. Almost nobody understood what modern weapons — machine guns, artillery, poison gas — would actually do to human bodies on a real battlefield.

### Part 2: The Great War — World War I (1914–1918)

On June 28, 1914, a nineteen-year-old Serbian nationalist named Gavrilo Princip shot and killed Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary in Sarajevo. Within six weeks, because of the alliance system, nearly all of Europe was at war.

The war that everyone expected to last a few months lasted four years and killed between 15 and 22 million people.

**The Western Front:** In France and Belgium, the armies dug trenches — long ditches in the earth, stretching from the English Channel to Switzerland. Soldiers lived in these trenches for months and years. The space between the opposing trenches was called No Man's Land — a blasted, cratered wasteland of mud, barbed wire, and unburied bodies. Attacks meant climbing out of your trench and running across No Man's Land toward machine guns. Thousands of men would die to move the front line a few hundred yards. Then the other side would counterattack and take it back.

The Battle of the Somme in 1916: on the first day alone, the British army suffered nearly 60,000 casualties — killed, wounded, or missing. The battle lasted five months. The front line moved approximately six miles.

**What was new:** This was the first industrial war. Machine guns, which could fire hundreds of rounds per minute, made traditional infantry charges suicidal. Artillery — massive guns firing explosive shells — could kill from miles away. Poison gas — chlorine, phosgene, mustard gas — was used for the first time, burning lungs and blinding eyes. Tanks and airplanes appeared for the first time on battlefields. War had been transformed by the same industrial technology that built the modern world.

**The human cost:** Soldiers who survived the fighting often could not recover from what they had seen. They called it "shell shock" — what we now call post-traumatic stress. An entire generation of young European men was destroyed or damaged. The phrase "the Lost Generation" described not just those who died but those who came home unable to find meaning in the world that had sent them to die.

**How it ended:** By 1918, Germany and its allies were exhausted. The entry of the United States in 1917 tipped the balance. Germany surrendered on November 11, 1918. The Treaty of Versailles imposed harsh punishment on Germany — enormous financial payments, loss of territory, limits on its military, and a clause that declared Germany solely responsible for the war. These punishments were so severe that they planted the seeds of the next, far worse war.

### Part 3: Between the Wars — The Rise of Dangerous Ideas

The twenty years between the two world wars (1918–1939) were years of chaos, suffering, and the rise of leaders who promised desperate people a way out — and delivered catastrophe.

**Russia becomes the Soviet Union:** In 1917, while World War I was still raging, Russia had a revolution. The Bolsheviks, led by Vladimir Lenin, seized power and created the world's first communist state. Communism promised equality — no rich, no poor, everything shared. What it delivered was a dictatorship. Lenin's secret police, the Cheka, killed thousands of perceived enemies. When Lenin died in 1924, Joseph Stalin took power and built one of the most murderous regimes in human history.

**Stalin's Soviet Union:** Stalin forced millions of peasant farmers onto collective farms controlled by the government. Those who resisted were killed, imprisoned, or sent to labor camps called gulags. The gulags were a vast system of prison camps — eventually holding millions of people — in the coldest, most remote parts of Russia. Prisoners were worked to death, frozen, starved. Estimates of total deaths in Stalin's gulags range from 1.5 to 6 million or more.

**The Holodomor (1932–1933):** Stalin imposed grain quotas on Ukraine that required farmers to give nearly all their food to the government. When Ukrainians could not meet the quotas, soldiers came and took everything — every grain of wheat, every potato, every animal. People who tried to flee were shot. An estimated 3.5 to 7.5 million Ukrainians died of deliberate starvation. The word "Holodomor" means "death by hunger" in Ukrainian. This was not a natural famine. It was a man-made catastrophe — a government deliberately starving its own people. Many countries now recognize the Holodomor as genocide.

**Germany turns to fascism:** Germany after World War I was humiliated, impoverished, and angry. The economy collapsed. Money became nearly worthless — people needed wheelbarrows of cash to buy bread. In this desperation, Adolf Hitler and the Nazi Party rose to power by 1933. Hitler promised to restore German greatness, to find someone to blame for Germany's suffering, and to build a thousand-year empire. The people he blamed were the Jews.

**Italy and Japan:** Benito Mussolini seized power in Italy in 1922, creating the first fascist state. Japan's military increasingly controlled its government and pursued imperial expansion in Asia, invading Manchuria in 1931 and China in 1937 — committing horrific atrocities, including the Nanjing Massacre, where Japanese soldiers murdered an estimated 200,000 to 300,000 Chinese civilians and prisoners of war in a matter of weeks.

### Part 4: The Armenian Genocide — The Warning the World Ignored

Before the Holocaust, there was another genocide that the world chose to look away from. During World War I, the Ottoman Empire (modern-day Turkey) systematically murdered its Armenian Christian population. Beginning in 1915, Armenian men were rounded up and shot. Women, children, and the elderly were forced on death marches into the Syrian desert without food or water. An estimated 600,000 to 1.5 million Armenians were killed.

This matters for a specific reason beyond the horror itself: the world's failure to respond to the Armenian Genocide taught future dictators that genocide could be committed without consequence. In 1939, as Hitler prepared his own plans for mass murder, he said: "Who, after all, speaks today of the annihilation of the Armenians?" He was right — almost nobody did. The lesson was clear: you could destroy a people, and the world would forget.

Turkey still does not officially recognize the Armenian Genocide. The political reasons for this — and the fact that major nations delayed recognition for decades to preserve their relationship with Turkey — is itself a lesson in how politics shapes which atrocities get remembered.

### Part 5: World War II — The Most Destructive War in Human History (1939–1945)

World War II killed between 70 and 85 million people. That is a number almost impossible to comprehend. It was fought on every continent except Antarctica and on every ocean. It was the largest, deadliest, most destructive conflict in the entire history of the human species.

**How it began:** Hitler invaded Poland on September 1, 1939. Britain and France declared war on Germany. Over the next two years, Germany conquered most of Europe with stunning speed — France fell in six weeks. Japan, allied with Germany and Italy (the "Axis" powers), expanded its empire across Asia and the Pacific.

**Total war:** This was not a war fought only between armies. Entire civilian populations were targets. Germany bombed London relentlessly during the Blitz. Britain and the United States fire-bombed German cities — the bombing of Dresden in February 1945 created a firestorm that killed an estimated 25,000 people in a single night, most of them civilians. The fire-bombing of Tokyo on March 9–10, 1945, killed an estimated 80,000 to 100,000 Japanese civilians in one night — more than the immediate deaths from either atomic bomb. War had become something that happened to everyone, not just soldiers.

**The Eastern Front:** The war between Germany and the Soviet Union was the largest and most brutal theater of the entire war. Germany invaded the Soviet Union in June 1941. The fighting lasted nearly four years, killed an estimated 27 million Soviet citizens (both military and civilian), and was characterized by deliberate brutality on both sides. The Siege of Leningrad lasted 872 days — nearly two and a half years — during which an estimated 800,000 civilians died, mostly of starvation.

### Part 6: The Holocaust — The Systematic Murder of a People

The Holocaust was the deliberate, systematic, industrialized murder of six million Jews — approximately one-third of all the Jewish people in the world — by Nazi Germany and its collaborators.

**It did not happen all at once.** It happened in stages, each one making the next seem almost inevitable:

First came the laws. In 1935, the Nuremberg Laws stripped German Jews of their citizenship. Jews could not marry non-Jews. Jews could not hold government jobs. Jewish children were expelled from schools. Jewish businesses were boycotted and then seized.

Then came the violence. On November 9, 1938 — Kristallnacht, the "Night of Broken Glass" — Nazi mobs attacked Jewish homes, businesses, and synagogues across Germany. Thousands of Jewish men were arrested and sent to concentration camps.

Then came the ghettos. Jews were forced into walled-off sections of cities — the Warsaw Ghetto held 400,000 people in an area of 1.3 square miles. People starved. Disease spread. Thousands died before the killing camps even began operating.

Then came the systematic murder. At the Wannsee Conference in January 1942, senior Nazi officials met to coordinate what they called "the Final Solution to the Jewish Question." They were bureaucrats — civil servants with briefcases and agendas — planning the logistics of murdering every Jew in Europe. Trains would carry people to camps. Gas chambers would kill them. Crematoria would burn the bodies. It was murder organized like a factory.

The camps — Auschwitz, Treblinka, Sobibor, Belzec, Majdanek, Chelmno — were death factories. People arrived on trains, were sorted — those who could work were enslaved, the rest were sent immediately to the gas chambers. At Auschwitz alone, over one million people were murdered.

**Anne Frank** was one of them. She was a girl who lived in hiding in Amsterdam for two years with her family, writing in her diary about her fears, her hopes, the boy she liked, her arguments with her mother. She was discovered, arrested, and sent to Bergen-Belsen concentration camp, where she died of typhus in February or March 1945 — just weeks before the camp was liberated. She was fifteen years old.

**Who participated:** The Holocaust was not carried out only by a small group of fanatics. It required the participation of tens of thousands of ordinary people — train conductors who drove the trains, clerks who processed the paperwork, soldiers who guarded the camps, neighbors who reported hiding Jews. Understanding how ordinary people participated in extraordinary evil is one of the most important and most disturbing lessons of this period.

### Part 7: The Porajmos — The Forgotten Genocide

The Nazis did not only murder Jews. They also systematically murdered the Roma people — the Romani, sometimes called "Gypsies," a people originally from India who had lived in Europe for centuries.

An estimated 500,000 to 1.5 million Roma were murdered during the Nazi period. Proportionally, the Roma may have suffered the highest death rate of any ethnic group in Nazi-occupied Europe.

The Roma genocide is called the Porajmos — "the Devouring" in the Romani language. It is almost never taught in schools. There are specific reasons for this: Roma communities had little political power after the war, their testimony was not prioritized at the Nuremberg trials, documentation was sparse because the Roma were largely an oral culture, and nobody advocated for their inclusion in the official memory of the war.

The fact that an entire genocide can happen and then be largely forgotten by the educational systems of the countries where it happened is itself one of the most important things to understand about how history works. What gets remembered is not always what was largest or most terrible. It is often what had the most powerful advocates afterward.

### Part 8: The Soviet System — Evil on the Other Side

While Nazi Germany was committing its atrocities, the Soviet Union under Stalin was committing its own — on an enormous scale. It is essential to understand that the evil of the twentieth century was not limited to one political movement. Fascism and communism — ideologies that claimed to be complete opposites — both produced mass death.

**The gulags** held millions of prisoners over decades. People were sent to the gulags for being the wrong social class, for telling the wrong joke, for being denounced by a neighbor. They worked in mines, built canals, cut timber in Siberian winters. They froze, starved, and were worked to death. The Gulag Archipelago — the phrase coined by Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn, a prisoner himself — described a chain of camps stretching across the entire Soviet Union like islands in an ocean.

**The Great Terror (1936–1938):** Stalin became convinced — or pretended to be convinced — that enemies were everywhere. In the Great Terror, an estimated 750,000 people were executed and over a million imprisoned. Senior Communist Party leaders, generals, scientists, writers — anyone could be arrested. Confessions were extracted through torture. People confessed to crimes that were physically impossible. The trials were performances designed to demonstrate that no one was safe.

**Why this matters alongside the Holocaust:** Both the Nazi and Soviet systems demonstrate what happens when a government has total power over its citizens and an ideology that justifies using that power without moral limits. The mechanisms are different — the Nazis organized industrial killing based on race; the Soviets organized systematic imprisonment and starvation based on class — but the result is the same: millions of human beings murdered by their own governments. A student who learns about only one of these systems has an incomplete picture of the twentieth century's lesson about unchecked power.

### Part 9: The Atomic Bomb — The Power to End Everything

On August 6, 1945, the United States dropped an atomic bomb on the Japanese city of Hiroshima. In a single flash, approximately 80,000 people were killed instantly. Tens of thousands more died in the following weeks and months from burns, radiation sickness, and injuries. Three days later, a second atomic bomb was dropped on Nagasaki, killing approximately 40,000 people instantly.

Japan surrendered on August 15, 1945. World War II was over.

**Sadako Sasaki** was two years old when the Hiroshima bomb exploded. She survived the blast but developed leukemia ten years later from the radiation. While in the hospital, she began folding paper cranes — according to Japanese tradition, anyone who folds a thousand cranes will be granted a wish. She wished to live. She folded over a thousand cranes. She died on October 25, 1955, at age twelve.

**The moral question:** The atomic bombings ended the war — but they killed over 100,000 civilians instantly, with tens of thousands more dying afterward. Were they justified? Some argue they saved more lives than they took by ending the war without an invasion of Japan that might have killed millions. Others argue that deliberately incinerating cities full of civilians can never be justified, regardless of the outcome. This is a question honest people disagree about, and you should think about it seriously rather than accepting any easy answer.

**What the bomb changed forever:** After Hiroshima, humanity possessed the power to destroy itself. This had never been true before. Within a few years, the Soviet Union also had atomic bombs. The two superpowers built arsenals capable of destroying all human civilization many times over. The world entered the age of nuclear weapons — and has never left it. Every day since August 6, 1945, humanity has lived with the possibility of its own extinction.

### Part 10: The Beginning of the Cold War — A World Divided

After World War II, the alliance between the United States and the Soviet Union collapsed. They had fought together against Hitler, but they agreed about almost nothing else. The United States believed in democracy and capitalism. The Soviet Union believed in communism and single-party rule.

Europe was divided. The western half — freed by American, British, and French forces — became democratic. The eastern half — occupied by the Soviet Red Army — became communist, controlled by Moscow. Germany itself was split in two: West Germany and East Germany. The city of Berlin, deep inside East Germany, was also divided. In 1961, the Soviet-backed East German government built a wall through the middle of Berlin to stop its own citizens from escaping to the West. The Berlin Wall became the most powerful symbol of the Cold War — a wall built not to keep enemies out, but to keep your own people in.

The Cold War was not a traditional war with battles. It was a contest of ideologies, fought through espionage, propaganda, proxy wars in other countries, and the ever-present threat of nuclear annihilation. It lasted over forty years and shaped the world you live in.

### Part 11: Mao's China — The Great Leap Forward

The horrors of the twentieth century were not limited to Europe. In China, Mao Zedong's Communist Party took power in 1949 and proceeded to commit some of the largest-scale atrocities in human history.

**The Great Leap Forward (1958–1962):** Mao decided to rapidly industrialize China through centrally planned farming and manufacturing. Local officials, terrified of reporting failure, lied about crop yields — reporting harvests many times larger than reality. Based on these false reports, the government took enormous amounts of grain from the countryside. The result was the worst famine in human history: an estimated 45 to 55 million people died of starvation. This was not a natural disaster. It was a man-made catastrophe caused by ideology, dishonesty, and a government with total power and no accountability.

This is worth knowing alongside the Holodomor and the Holocaust because it demonstrates the same lesson from yet another direction: when governments have total control over their citizens and are guided by ideology rather than reality, the result is mass death — regardless of which ideology is in charge.

### Part 12: What This Period Teaches

The twentieth century taught humanity lessons that must never be forgotten:

**Governments can be the greatest threat to their own people.** The deadliest forces in the twentieth century were not foreign invaders — they were governments murdering their own citizens or citizens of conquered territories. Nazi Germany, the Soviet Union, Mao's China, the Ottoman Empire during the Armenian Genocide — in each case, the state itself was the killer.

**Ideology makes atrocity possible.** Every mass murder of the twentieth century was justified by an idea — racial purity, class revolution, national destiny. The ideas were different but the mechanism was the same: once you convince yourself that a group of people is less than human, or that history requires their elimination, ordinary moral rules stop applying.

**Ordinary people participate.** The Holocaust, the gulags, the Cultural Revolution — none of these were carried out by small groups of psychopaths. They were carried out by millions of ordinary human beings who convinced themselves, or allowed themselves to be convinced, that what they were doing was acceptable. Understanding this is far more disturbing — and far more important — than believing that only monsters commit atrocities.

**What is remembered is a choice.** The Holocaust is well-known. The Holodomor is barely taught. The Porajmos is almost completely unknown. The Armenian Genocide is still disputed by Turkey. What societies choose to remember and teach about their past is itself a political act. The student who knows this is harder to manipulate.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Read-Through [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
Read the relevant chapters of *The Story of the World, Volume 4* aloud, in order. After each chapter, the student narrates back what they heard. Supplement with Eyewitness pages, photographs, and maps. The narration is the assessment.

### Study 2: The Trench Experience [Early in the Packet]
Using books, photographs, and if available documentary footage, study the Western Front trenches. What did soldiers eat? Where did they sleep? What did No Man's Land look like? What sounds did they hear? The student narrates what it would have been like to be a soldier in the trenches. Key question: these soldiers went to war expecting it to be glorious. What did they find instead?

### Study 3: Anne Frank's Diary [Mid-Packet]
Read selected entries from Anne Frank's diary aloud. Before reading, establish the context: who Anne was, why she was hiding, what was happening to Jewish people. After reading, discuss: What was Anne like as a person? What did she worry about? What did she hope for? What happened to her? How old would she be today if she had survived?

### Study 4: Mapping the Wars [Mid-Packet]
On a large map, trace the extent of both World Wars. For WWI: the Western Front, the Eastern Front, the Ottoman theaters. For WWII: the European theater, the Pacific theater, North Africa. Mark key locations: Sarajevo, the Somme, Stalingrad, Normandy, Hiroshima, Auschwitz. The student should see that these were truly global wars.

### Study 5: Two Terrors — Nazi and Soviet [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Study the Holocaust and the Soviet gulag system side by side. Key questions: How were they different? (Racial ideology versus class ideology; death camps versus labor camps.) How were they similar? (Government with total power; ideology that justified killing; ordinary people participating.) Why is one much more well-known than the other? What does that tell us about how history gets told?

### Study 6: The Forgotten Victims [Late Packet]
Dedicated study of the Porajmos, the Armenian Genocide, and the Holodomor. For each: What happened? How many people died? Why is it less well-known than the Holocaust? Key discussion: Why do some genocides get remembered and others are almost forgotten? Is this fair? What could change it?

### Study 7: Sadako and the Bomb [Late Packet]
Read *Sadako and the Thousand Paper Cranes*. After reading, discuss: What happened to Sadako? Why did she get sick ten years after the bomb? What did she try to do? Fold paper cranes together. Then discuss the bigger question: The bomb ended the war. But it killed tens of thousands of people like Sadako. How do you think about that?

### Study 8: The Cold War Wall [End of Packet]
Study the Berlin Wall. What was it? Why was it built? What happened to people who tried to cross it? What does it tell you about a government that has to build a wall to keep its own people from leaving? Connect to the beginning of the Cold War: two superpowers, two ideologies, a world divided.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Narrative Comprehension
**Developing well:** The student can retell the major events of the world wars, the Holocaust, the Soviet atrocities, and the atomic bombings with specific detail and a sense of chronological order. Narrations are getting richer over the course of the packet.

**Developing slowly:** The student can only retell vague generalities — "bad things happened in the war" — without specific people, places, or events. Narrations lack detail despite multiple readings.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the reading material at the right level? (Some Stretching Branches students may need more visual and less text.) Are you narrating back to the student first, modeling what detailed retelling looks like? Is the pace too fast — moving to new material before the previous reading has been absorbed?

### 2. Moral Engagement
**Developing well:** The student expresses genuine feeling about the material — sadness, anger, confusion, moral outrage. They ask "why" questions. They connect the material to their own sense of right and wrong. They identify with the victims and/or the rescuers.

**Developing slowly:** The student treats the material as abstract or distant — "that was a long time ago" — without emotional engagement. Alternatively, the student is overwhelmed and avoids engagement.

**Diagnostic questions:** For disengagement: are you modeling engagement yourself? Are you reading with seriousness and feeling? For overwhelm: have you slowed down and created more processing space? Is the student getting enough time to talk about what they are feeling?

### 3. Multi-Directional Understanding of Evil
**Developing well:** The student understands that atrocity was committed by multiple political systems — not just the Nazis. They can identify at least one Soviet atrocity and articulate that bad governments come in multiple forms.

**Developing slowly:** The student identifies evil only with Nazis/Germany and has no awareness of Soviet or other atrocities. Or the student has absorbed the idea that "war is bad" as an undifferentiated generality without understanding specific events.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you given Soviet atrocities as much reading and discussion time as Nazi atrocities? If the curriculum has been unbalanced, the student's understanding will reflect that imbalance.

### 4. Awareness of Forgotten Victims
**Developing well:** The student can name at least one genocide or atrocity beyond the Holocaust and articulate why it receives less attention. They begin to understand that what is taught and remembered is a choice, not a neutral reflection of what happened.

**Developing slowly:** The student knows about the Holocaust but is unaware of the Porajmos, the Armenian Genocide, or the Holodomor.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you dedicated reading and discussion time specifically to the forgotten victims? If these were covered briefly at the end of the packet, the student will treat them as footnotes — which is exactly the problem the packet is designed to correct.

### 5. The Atomic Bomb as a Turning Point
**Developing well:** The student understands that the atomic bomb changed the world permanently — that after Hiroshima, humanity possessed the power to destroy itself. They can articulate the moral question (the bomb ended the war but killed tens of thousands of civilians) without forcing a simplistic answer.

**Developing slowly:** The student sees the bomb as just another weapon or just another bad thing that happened during the war, without understanding what made it different.

**Diagnostic questions:** Did you spend enough time on Sadako's story and the concept of nuclear weapons? The emotional and conceptual weight of the bomb requires dedicated attention — it cannot be absorbed as one event among many.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Holocaust Dominance
The Holocaust is the best-documented, most culturally prominent atrocity in this packet. It is easy for it to consume all the available time and attention. Guard against this. The Holodomor, the Porajmos, the Armenian Genocide, and Soviet gulags each need real time. A student who "did World War II" but only knows about the Holocaust has received incomplete education — and, critically, has absorbed the structural bias this packet is designed to correct.

### Avoidance of Soviet Material
Many mentors are more familiar and more comfortable with WWII/Holocaust material than with Soviet atrocities. If you find yourself spending three weeks on WWII and one session on the Soviet Union, correct the imbalance. Read the mentor resources. The Holodomor and the gulags are as important to this packet as the Holocaust.

### Premature Moral Complexity
At Stretching Branches, the student needs moral clarity first: these things were evil, these governments were tyrannical, these people suffered terribly. The analytical complexity — how ordinary people participate in atrocity, the institutional dynamics of genocide, the ideological genealogy — belongs to Weathering Storms and C. If you introduce too much complexity too early, the student may lose the moral foundation that the analysis is built on.

### Using the Material for Indoctrination
This material can easily be weaponized: "This is what happens when government gets too big" or "This is what happens when people are racist." Both claims contain truth, but neither is the lesson. The lesson is: unchecked power combined with dehumanizing ideology produces mass death, regardless of which ideology or which type of government. Do not use the student's emotional response to this material to install a specific political conclusion. Give them the facts, let them feel what the facts provoke, and trust that the honest encounter with the material will produce its own moral formation.

### Skipping the Atomic Bomb's Moral Question
It is tempting to present the atomic bomb as simply "the thing that ended the war." It is also tempting to present it as simply "an atrocity." Neither is adequate. The student should encounter the genuine tension: the bomb killed tens of thousands of civilians, and it may have prevented even more deaths. This is one of the first genuine moral dilemmas the student will encounter in the curriculum. Do not resolve it for them.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: World War I
**Strong:** Retells the story with specific detail — the assassination, the alliance system, the trenches, the scale of casualties. May include emotional response: "It must have been terrible." Shows understanding that the war was worse than anyone expected.
**Weak:** "There was a big war in Europe." No detail, no sense of what made WWI distinctive.

### Prompt 2: The Holocaust
**Strong:** Describes the step-by-step process — laws, exclusion, ghettos, camps. Names Anne Frank and tells her story with feeling. Understands that millions of people were murdered. May express moral outrage.
**Weak:** "The Nazis were bad and killed people." No specifics, no process, no personal connection.

### Prompt 3: Soviet Atrocities
**Strong:** Can describe the Holodomor or the gulags with specific detail. Understands that the Soviet government, not just Nazi Germany, committed mass murder. Can articulate that evil is not limited to one political system.
**Weak:** Has no knowledge of Soviet atrocities, or can only say "Russia was bad too" without specifics.

### Prompt 4: Forgotten Genocides
**Strong:** Names a specific forgotten genocide and describes what happened. Can articulate why it is less known — some version of "nobody told people about it" or "the people who survived did not have enough power to make people listen."
**Weak:** Cannot name any genocide beyond the Holocaust.

### Prompt 5: The Atomic Bomb
**Strong:** Tells the story of Hiroshima and/or Sadako with specific detail. Understands that the bomb was different from other weapons. Can articulate the moral tension without forcing a simple answer.
**Weak:** Treats the bomb as just another weapon or just another bad event.

### Prompt 6: Why These Things Happened
**Strong:** Attempts an explanation that goes beyond "bad people did bad things" — mentions ideology, government power, scapegoating, or the failure of others to stop it. Shows the beginning of causal thinking.
**Weak:** "Because people are mean" or "I don't know." No attempt at causal reasoning.

### Prompt 7: What They Learned
**Strong:** Articulates a specific lesson with personal conviction. May connect to their own life or values. Shows that the material has affected them intellectually and emotionally.
**Weak:** "It was sad" or "War is bad" — generic responses without personal engagement.
